change_1_cardoverlay_role_img.evidence: dot.setAttribute('role', 'img') present at line 106, immediately followed by aria-label at line 115 and title at line 114. Both attributes carry the same dotLabel string.

evidence.addPromise: lines 301-309 — immutable spread, pushes {id, madeAtCard: s.run.cardsPlayed, deadlineCards, status:'active'} to run.promises array
evidence.fulfillPromise: lines 311-318 — map over promises, sets status:'fulfilled' only when id matches AND status==='active'
evidence.breakPromise: lines 320-327 — same guard pattern, sets status:'broken'

evidence.ADD_PROMISE: lines 141-146 — parses id=args[0], deadline=parseInt(args[1]??'5') with fallback 5, calls store.getState().addPromise()
evidence.VALID_CODES_registration: ADD_PROMISE:2, FULFILL_PROMISE:1, BREAK_PROMISE:1 registered at lines 21-23
evidence.UNLOCK_OGHAM_no_op: UNLOCK_OGHAM falls through to default no-op as before — correct, not part of this commit

notes: All three implementations use immutable spread — no mutation anywhere.
fulfillPromise and breakPromise both guard on status==='active', preventing double-state transitions.
ADD_PROMISE deadline fallback (parseInt ?? 5) handles malformed card data gracefully.
FULFILL_PROMISE/BREAK_PROMISE on unknown id silently no-ops (map returns array unchanged) — acceptable for a system without server-side validation.
Minor: FULFILL_PROMISE passes args[0]??'' to fulfillPromise — empty-string id could spuriously match a promise whose id was also '' if card data is badly authored. Low-probability but worth noting.
UNLOCK_OGHAM correctly remains a no-op (default branch) and emits a console.warn.
Promise state is now correctly tracked: active/fulfilled/broken/expired statuses defined in the Promise type (line 46).

EDGE CASE 1 — zero-deadline promise: ADD_PROMISE:someId:0 → parseInt returns 0, fallback '|| 5' kicks in, so deadline defaults to 5. This silently corrects a card-authoring mistake. Semantically correct but no warning is emitted — card authors won't know their '0' was overridden.

EDGE CASE 2 — negative deadline: ADD_PROMISE:someId:-3 → parseInt returns -3, '|| 5' does NOT fire (since -3 is truthy), so deadlineCards=-3 enters state. No card in run can check for expiry yet (expiry logic not implemented), so this is a dead value; harmless now but a future expiry checker could produce unexpected behavior.

EDGE CASE 3 — duplicate promise id: ADD_PROMISE can push duplicate ids with no dedup check. fulfillPromise/breakPromise will only affect the first matching active instance via map. The rest remain active. This is a silent accumulation risk.
EDGE CASE 4 — FULFILL on non-existent id: silently no-ops. Acceptable for now.

The 'expired' status is declared in the Promise interface (line 46) but no transition to 'expired' is implemented anywhere in EffectEngine or Store. This is a declared-but-dead status — promises currently never expire by time, only by explicit FULFILL/BREAK effects.
Promise data enriches state coherence and lays the foundation for consequence chains. The core mechanic is balanced in intent; the missing expiry engine is the main gap.
role='img' on a <span> with aria-label satisfies WCAG 2.1 SC 1.1.1 (Non-text content) — screen readers will announce the label instead of trying to read an empty element.
The dotLabel format 'druides (+5)' is human-readable and informative.
title attribute is also set (line 114), providing tooltip for mouse users — redundant but harmless.

WCAG concern: the container <span> wrapping multiple dots (lines 122-125) has no role or aria-label of its own. Each individual dot has role='img'+aria-label, so screen readers will encounter a sequence of img elements without a grouping label. This is acceptable (SC 1.3.1 allows flat sequences) but a role='group' with aria-label='Faction impacts' on the container would be more ergonomic.
Overall this is a meaningful accessibility improvement over title-only. Passes WCAG 2.1 AA at the element level.
Promise tracking aligns with the GAME_DESIGN_BIBLE PROMISE effect system (bible section 13.3, pipeline step 10).
State shape (id, madeAtCard, deadlineCards, status) is well-structured for future narrative consequence triggers.
The 'expired' status gap (see equilibrage) means that currently a promise can only end in fulfilled or broken — a promise that simply lapses is invisible to the player.
Promises are tracked in state but NOT surfaced in RunSummary. The player cannot see at end of run whether they fulfilled or broke promises.
This DOES matter: the promise system is the primary driver of moral weight in MERLIN's narrative loop. Invisible promises contribute zero player emotion or sense of consequence.
RunSummary.ts exists (confirmed in file list) but no promise section was added in this commit.
However, the state foundation is correctly in place, so the UI exposure is a distinct task (C66 candidate). This commit is the correct sequencing — state before display.
Score reflects: technically correct sequence, but the feature is unplayable from the player's perspective until RunSummary is updated.
ADD_PROMISE arg parsing is defensive: args[0]??'' and parseInt(args[1]??'5',10)||5.
VALID_CODES declares ADD_PROMISE:2, so parseEffect() will reject ADD_PROMISE:id (missing deadline) and ADD_PROMISE:id:deadline:extra (too many args). Correct enforcement.
If LLM generates ADD_PROMISE:: (empty id and empty deadline) → id='', deadline=5 by fallback. No crash, no state corruption. Acceptable resilience.

The promise id namespace is fully LLM-controlled with no registry validation — a hallucinated id will silently push to state and never match a FULFILL unless the same hallucination repeats exactly. This is inherent to the open-id design and not a regression.
All three implementations follow the established immutable pattern (spread on run object).
fulfillPromise and breakPromise correctly scope the guard to status==='active' — prevents re-breaking an already-broken promise.
No new console.log statements. console.warn for no-op effects remains appropriate.
EffectEngine correctly uses store.getState() (not a stale closure) for all three cases.
The Promise interface uses readonly on all fields — enforces immutability at the type level.
Promise array operations are O(n) map() — negligible for typical run length (15-40 cards, each with 0-3 promises).
No DOM operations introduced in Store or EffectEngine.
role='img' setAttribute is a single DOM call per dot, no performance concern.
No minigame changes in this commit — score carries forward from C64.
Promise effects can in principle be triggered by any card, including those attached to minigame outcomes. No integration tested.
Three focused, well-scoped changes. No cross-file side effects observed.
TypeScript types are consistent: Promise interface in Store.ts matches usage in EffectEngine.ts.
No new dependencies introduced.

composite_score.delta_note: Score dip is expected: promise state is implemented but not player-visible yet. The -0.4 is entirely attributable to progression_fun_factor (6.5) and the equilibrage edge cases. Technical quality is high.

verdict_rationale: No critical bugs. All three changes are correctly implemented with good defensive coding. The promise system is a sound foundation. Score drop from 8.3 to 7.9 reflects completeness gap (player-invisible promises, missing expiry logic) rather than any regression.

issues.issue: Negative deadline not sanitized — ADD_PROMISE:id:-3 stores deadlineCards=-3 in state with no warning. Future expiry logic will behave unexpectedly.
issues.recommendation: Add Math.max(1, deadline) guard in EffectEngine ADD_PROMISE case, or in Store.addPromise(). Emit console.warn if original value was <= 0.

issues.issue: 'expired' status declared in Promise interface but no transition path exists. Promises can only end as 'active', 'fulfilled', or 'broken' — 'expired' is a dead type state.
issues.recommendation: Either remove 'expired' from the union until the expiry engine is built, or implement expiry check in tickCooldowns() / incrementCardsPlayed().

issues.issue: Promises tracked in run state but not rendered in RunSummary — player has no feedback on promise outcomes at run end.
issues.recommendation: Add a Promises section to RunSummary.ts showing fulfilled/broken counts with moral framing. This is the highest-value unblocked C66 task.

issues.issue: No deduplication guard on addPromise — duplicate ids can accumulate. fulfillPromise/breakPromise affect only the first matching active instance.
issues.recommendation: Consider replacing push with an upsert: if a promise with the same id exists and is active, overwrite its deadlineCards rather than duplicating. Document intended behavior in a code comment.

issues.issue: Multi-dot container span has no role or aria-label. Screen readers traverse dots as a flat sequence of img elements without grouping context.
issues.recommendation: Add role='group' and aria-label='Impacts de faction' to the container span when dots.length > 1.

top3_c66.rationale: Highest fun-factor gain per dev effort. Promises in state, display layer missing. One focused UI component. Unblocks the moral consequence loop.

top3_c66.task: Promise expiry engine — implement 'expired' status transition in tickCooldowns() or a new checkPromiseExpiry() action
top3_c66.rationale: Closes the dead-status gap. Without expiry, promises are only resolved by explicit card effects; most will remain 'active' forever, diluting the system's meaning.

top3_c66.task: Negative deadline sanitization + multi-dot ARIA grouping
top3_c66.rationale: Two small defensive fixes bundled: Math.max(1, deadline) guard + role='group' on the dot container. Combined they close the two LOW issues and raise accessibilite and equilibrage marginally.

balance_observations: Promise system introduces no new numerical parameters — no balance tuning needed at this stage.

Expiry mechanic (rank-2 task) will introduce a deadline window parameter; recommend starting with deadlineCards=5 as the default (already baked into the EffectEngine fallback) and tuning via playtesting.
fulfillPromise/breakPromise effects should eventually trigger secondary effects (reputation delta, anam gain/loss) to give promises mechanical weight beyond narrative tracking.
